So you can see over here, we call it an overall match score.
Uh These scores that go from 0 to 100 100 would be the best match or the best comp ever.
A zero would be the worst.
But our algorithm base these scores off of things like location.

So it's a great way to identify new comps as well as see how strong a particular competitor might be.

Um I don't know if you remember this or not, but advertised occupancy.
So the reason we call this advertised occupancy instead of just regular occupancy is because it's based off of the known number of vacant units in the property.
And what I mean by that has to do with how we collect our data.
So let's pretend that a property out there has 31 bedrooms available in the property.
However, on the website, on their online leasing, they decide to only make 10 of those available.
Our system is only able to account for the 10 that they made available.
We have no clue that those other 20 exist.

If you think about it from the renter's point of view, you know, he or she visits that property website, they're looking to rent a one bedroom, all that they know that exists are those 10.
Therefore, those 10 are exactly what's impacting their decision making.
And those 10 are what's kind of dictating in the market in terms of, of of price, price per foot square footage and all that other good stuff.
